title: Sanitizers, Static Analysis, and Fuzzing · Pointer Forensics
summary: An incident responder inherits a corrupted layered memory-defect test matrix, the earliest failing input, and two plausible hypotheses; they must preserve first-failure evidence, repair the cause, and add a regression. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in layered memory-defect test matrix and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Build and run representative tests with AddressSanitizer and LeakSanitizer, symbols, fatal findings, and supported-target notes.
Use UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer and fully instrumented MemorySanitizer environments for their distinct defect classes.
Run Clang and GCC static analyzers, preserve paths and assumptions, classify findings, and regression-test repairs.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record · Debug Asan Lsan Evidence
steps.instruction:
debug case — A debug native-systems team handles memory case 3691. Produce bounded C evidence to classify a defect against complementary native tools; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Build and run representative tests with AddressSanitizer and LeakSanitizer, symbols, fatal findings, and supported-target notes.

Inspect the Asan Lsan Evidence trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?

steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair · Debug Tool Limit Matrix
steps.instruction:
For this debug demonstration of cmem-tool-limit-matrix, write the complete compile-ready C program headed "/* Evidence: cmem-tool-limit-matrix-lrbhqv */" for changed case 9074. It must classify a defect against complementary native tools, reject one changed boundary, print an observable result and invariant, and return nonzero when the invariant fails. Keep instant code within the PicoC 3.2.2 practice subset; do not claim C23, optimizing compiler, sanitizer, ABI, thread, operating-system, FFI, load, or production evidence until its explicit controlled transfer gate passes.

hints: For Publish the incident record · Debug Tool Limit Matrix, name the size, bounds, object, lifetime, owner, borrow, allocation, cleanup, representation, ABI, thread, and transfer boundary, Tool Limit Matrix precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Publish the incident record · Debug Tool Limit Matrix, trace this concrete input and predict size arithmetic, pointer range, lifetime transition, allocation result, cleanup order, printed output, and native evidence still missing before reviewing it: A debug native-systems team handles memory case 6547. Produce bounded C evidence to classify a defect against complementary native tools; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Maintain a defect-by-tool and platform matrix so unsupported configurations and blind spots remain explicit.

For Publish the incident record · Debug Tool Limit Matrix, challenge the documented misconception with changed-size, failure-injection, printed invariant, compiler, sanitizer, analyzer, fuzz, ABI, concurrency, or production-transfer evidence: Running every tool once is better than selecting compatible complementary gates.
